message.group-title: Intensive Care and Critical Care Medicine
message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In physiological conditions arterial blood lactate concentration is equal or lower than central venous. The aim of this study was to explore if the COVID-19 induced lung cells damage was mirrored by an arterial lactatemia higher than the central venous one; then if the administration of immunosuppressant drugs (i.e. canakinumab) could normalize such abnormal lactate a-cv difference.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Prospective cohort study started on March 25
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    2020 for a duration of 10 days, enrolling 21 patients affected by severe COVID-19 pneumonia undergoing mechanical ventilation consecutively admitted to the ICU of the Rimini Hospital, Italy.
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:p>
                    Arterial and central venous blood samples were contemporary collected to calculate the difference between arterial and central venous lactate (Delta a-cv lactate) concentration within 24 h from tracheal intubation (T
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    ), and 24 hours after canakinumab administration (T
                    <jats:sub>1</jats:sub>
                    ).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    At the T
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    19/21 (90.5%) patients showed a pathologic Delta a-cv lactate (median 0.22 mmol/L; IQR 0.07–0.36), suggesting severe inflammation. In the 13 patients undergoing canakinumab administration, at the T
                    <jats:sub>1</jats:sub>
                    Delta a-cv lactate decreased in 92.3% of cases, being the decrease statistically significant (T
                    <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                    : median 0.15, IQR 0.07–0.25 mmol/L; T
                    <jats:sub>1</jats:sub>
                    : median −0.01, IQR 0.09–0.04 mmol/L; p=0.002).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A reversed Delta a-cv lactate is likely to be one of the effects of COVID-19 related cytokine storm, that could reflect a derangement in the lung cells mitochondrial metabolism induced by inflammation or other uncoupling mediators. Delta a-cv lactate decrease may reflect the anti-inflammatory activity of canakinumab. Our preliminary findings need to be confirmed by larger outcome studies.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Lactate arterial-central venous gradient among COVID-19 patients in ICU: a potential tool in the clinical practice

reference.article-title: Time course of blood lactate levels, inflammation, and mitochondrial function in experimental sepsis

reference.unstructured: Scott S , Antonaglia V , Guiotto G , Paladino F , Schiraldi F . Two-hour lactate clearance predicts negative outcome in patients with cardiorespiratory insufficiency. Crit Care Res Pract 2010; 2010: 917053.

reference.article-title: Lactate kinetics in ICU patients using a bolus of 13 C-labeled lactate
Lactate levels in arterial and venous blood may be correlated but not equivalent
